titleStmt.title: (Julius) Reinhard Oppel
Grünberg, Oberhessen, November 13, 1878; died Leipzig, November 21, 1941

publicationStmt.publisher: Schenker Documents Online in association with the Centre for Computing in the Humanities

sourceDesc.p: No source: created in electronic format.

encodingDesc.p: Encoding according to the CCH TEI Guidelines

div.p: German composer, music historian, theorist, and performer; friend of, and collaborator with, Heinrich Schenker.

div.head: Career Summary
div.p: Oppel was born in 1878 in Grünberg, 80km (50 miles) north of Frankfurt-am-Main, his father being a local school teacher and amateur musician. In 1884 the family moved to Groß-Gerau, south of Frankfurt and close to Darmstadt, where Reinhard attended the Gymnasium. During this period he became proficient in piano, organ, and violin. Thereafter, he studied at the Hoch Conservatory in Frankfurt (1897–1900) under the music theorist Iwan Knorr. At this time he also became acquainted with Arnold Mendelssohn, church music director in Darmstadt, who had a life-long influence upon him, and through him became acquainted with Josef Knettel, who was to be his closest friend and advocate. Oppel subsequently worked as organist and choirmaster at a Lutheran church near Bonn (1903–09), then completed a doctorate at the University of Munich with a dissertation on the Renaissance composer Jacob Meiland (1911).

From 1911 to 1924 he taught composition and theory at the Kiel Conservatory, his work there interrupted by war service on the eastern and western fronts (1914–19) – Schenker remarks that “Captain” Oppel “was decorated with the Iron Cross” and fought “in the trenches on the front line” (OJ 6/8, [9], December 24, 1931, to Moriz Violin). One of his colleagues at the Kiel Conservatory was Richard Glas, head of the piano masterclass, who became a long-term friend and exponent of and advocate for his compositions.

Having completed his Habilitation (Beiträge zur Melodielehre) in 1924, he taught music theory and history as a Privatdozent at the University of Kiel (1924–31). Overlapping with his time at Kiel was a post at the Leipzig Conservatory (1927–38), where one of his more gifted pupils was Theo Goos, who took a keen interest in Schenker’s theories, and where through the Leipzig Gewandhaus he befriended Wilhelm Furtwängler. At the Conservatory, where in Schenker’s words he was to “implement my system completely – harmony, counterpoint, analysis etc." (OJ 89/1, [2], August 12, 1927, to Anthony van Hoboken), he took a reactionary stance against the modernism of the “Reger-clique,” and in 1937/38 relinquished his position in protest at the removal from the University of Leipzig of another friend, philosophy professor Theodor Litt, by the National Socialists. In Leipzig itself, he played the organ at evangelical and catholic churches, and until 1938 one of its synagogues. Oppel was a keen swimmer and mountain hiker, and loved the Tyrol, something that he encouraged in his sons.

Oppel was married four times: to Käthe Brebeck (1906-10), Gertrud Bandelow (from 1911 until her death in 1925), Käthe Eggeling (1926–28), and Elfriede Dey (1930–41 at his death). He had a total of eight children, two of whom were adopted. (For further details, see Oppel family). Despite debilitating travel between Kiel and Leipzig in the overlap years 1927–1931, he was very much a family man; the correspondence documents a special fondness for his sons Ulrich, Walter and “Bepo” (Kurt). He had for some years suffered from angina pectoris and died in 1941 of a heart attack; his grave is in the main cemetery in Frankfurt.

div.head: Oppel and Schenker
div.p: Oppel was not formally a pupil of Heinrich Schenker: he appears in Schenker's lessonbooks as receiving instruction only between August 7 and 12, 1931 (OC 3/4), when he visited the Schenkers during their summer holiday, though he is mentioned there briefly in February 1914 (when Schenker seems to have shown Elias and Weisse an Oppel composition). However, his correspondence with Schenker had begun in October 1913, and contact between the two men was particularly strong in the 1920s and 1930s, and after Schenker's death he maintained the correspondence with Schenker’s widow (1935–39). As an avid and knowledgeable reader not only in the field of music but of philosophy, politics, history, and also of literature, he regularly drew Schenker’s attention to items that might interest him, sometimes sending him copies of books he had recently read.

Most particularly, Oppel assisted Schenker in the preparatory work for the latter's Brahms, Oktaven und Quinten u. a. (1929–33). He obtained Brahms autograph manuscript in August 1930 and set to work until January 1931 and again February to March, locating the cited passages of parallel fifths and octaves in published editions, commenting on them, conducting searches in libraries, and sending material to Schenker. Oppel and Schenker had a final consultation when Oppel visited Galtür in August 1931. The work was published in April 1933, with no acknowledgment of his contribution.

Oppel shared Schenker's belief in the place of aristocracy in society and his fervent nationalism. At times he expressed enthusiasm for Adolf Hitler’s actions – even as late as April 1938 (OJ 13/10, 198), when he welcomed the annexation of Austria to National-Socialist Germany (though he did this largely for personal reasons) – but he became disenchanted with German politics soon after the Nazis seized power. He appears to have resisted joining the Party, although in 1933 he served as the chair of a cell (Zellenobmann) of the party rewriting the history of the Leipzig Conservatory.

He was the first to give officially advertized university and conservatory courses in Schenkerian theory, in both Kiel and Leipzig, preceding by several years those given by Moriz Violin and Felix-Eberhard von Cube in Hamburg and Weisse in New York, and he planned, but never completed, a manual of Schenkerian analysis. He had aspirations to bring Schenker to Leipzig to teach, but circumstances in Leipzig prevented it.

div.head: Oppel as Composer and Historian
div.p: Oppel’s body of compositions embraced orchestral (all only fragmentary), chamber and solo instrumental music, song with piano accompaniment, and choral works sacred and secular. His music was taken seriously by several publishers and reached the public in concert and radio performances. His chamber music included four string quartets (No. 4 published by Edition Peters), widely promoted by the Schulze-Prisca Quartet, a String Quintet, a Wind Serenade (Op. 30), an Oboe Quartet, works for solo violin, solo viola, solo cello (Opp. 12, 14, 19), violin with piano (Opp. 20, 24, 36), sets of solo piano pieces (Opp. 21, 26, 27, 28 published by Breitkopf & Härtel, Opp. 30, and 31 published by Mühlau, some promoted by Richard Glas), and three piano sonatas. His vocal works included sets of solo songs, male-voice and mixed-voice secular choruses, and sacred choral works, some published by Vandehoeck & Ruprecht and several promoted by Josef Knettel in the Rhineland. Oppel also composed a set of waltzes for Jeanette Schenker in gratitude for her kindness, which she and Heinrich had decoratively bound as a sign of their appreciation of their artistic and personal value.
Oppel published articles on many Renaissance and Baroque subjects in journals including the Zeitschrift für Musikwissenschaft, the Neue Zeitschrift für Musik, and the Bach-Jahrbuch.

div.head: Correspondence with Schenker and Others
div.p: Correspondence with Heinrich Schenker spans 1913–35, and with Jeanette Schenker 1933–39, survives as OJ 13/10 (approximately 215 letters, postcards, and other items), and three other letters as OC 24/9 (1921), 52/637 and 52/640 (1925). Only one letter from Schenker to Oppel (from 1932) is known to survive; its length, at eleven pages (ROC K/5, 14), perhaps indicates the value Schenker placed on this correspondence. One letter survives from Oppel to Oswald Jonas as OJ 36/198, [1] (1935), and one letter from Oppel to Moriz Violin as OJ 70/30, [1] (1931). Seven photographs of him, with and without family, survive as OJ 72/11, ranging from 1907 to 1938.

Oppel's surviving papers, including compositions and substantial sets of correspondence to other recipients, are preserved in the Reinhard Oppel Collection at the University of North Texas, Denton, Willis Library (ROC).

listBibl.bibl: Articles in the Bach-Jahrbuch (of which Das Thema der Violinchaconne und seine Verwandten, Bach-Jahrbuch (1918), 97-116 is preserved as OJ 34/6)
Articles in the Neue Zeitschrift fur Musik
Articles in the Zeitschrift für Musikwissenschaft
(Julius) Reinard Oppel, in Schenker Traditionen: Eine Wiener Schule der Musiktheorie und ihre internationale Verbreitung, eds. M. Eybl & E. Fink-Mennel (Vienna: Böhlau, 2006), p. 244
Jackson, Timothy L., Heinrich Schenker as Composition Teacher: The Schenker-Oppel Exchange, Music Analysis 20/1 (March 2001), 1-115
